title: Profitable and Cash-Tight: The Working Capital Pattern That Growth Always Creates
linkedin:
The fastest-growing businesses sometimes have the tightest bank accounts. That's not a coincidence.

When revenue grows, the volume of work in your pipeline at any given moment grows with it. More outstanding invoices. More stock on hand. More costs committed before the corresponding revenue has been collected.

All of that needs to be funded before the additional revenue arrives in your account.

Here's the arithmetic: a business at $3 million annual revenue with 35-day debtor terms carries around $288,000 in receivables at any given time. Grow that business to $3.75 million and the receivables balance at the same debtor days reaches roughly $360,000. That extra $72,000 has to come from somewhere before a dollar of the growth revenue has landed.

The pattern is consistent. Most businesses growing above 15 per cent find the tightest cash period arrives five to ten weeks after growth activity accelerates — right around when Q4 superannuation falls due in late July, award wage increases land in early payrolls, and June debtors are still collecting.

The businesses that manage this well don't avoid the timing gap. They model it before it opens.

A 13-week cash flow forecast built in July shows the specific weeks where the balance falls. That timing window changes everything: you can approach your bank before the pressure arrives, accelerate collections from existing debtors, or phase growth to match your cash position.

Discovering the gap when it has already opened leaves only reactive options.

If the FY27 growth target is live and the cash model hasn't been built yet, that gap is worth closing before the funding gap is.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog. Link in the first comment.

facebook:
Something a lot of business owners notice in their best growth year: the bank account feels tighter than expected.

It's not because anything has gone wrong. It's because growth requires working capital upfront — before invoices are paid, before stock converts to sales, before the additional revenue actually lands.

We've just published a piece on why this happens, what the numbers look like, and how a forward cash model changes your options before the pressure arrives.

Have you experienced this in your own business? We'd love to hear how you managed it.

#CashFlow #SMEFinance
google:
How Revenue Growth Ties Up Cash Before It Creates It

If your FY27 revenue is tracking above last year, that's good news. It also means your business is building a working capital requirement that wasn't there twelve months ago.

When revenue grows, the volume of outstanding invoices, stock, and work-in-progress in motion at any given time grows with it. All of that needs to be funded before the additional revenue has been collected.

A business growing 25 per cent typically needs to fund around 25 per cent more working capital at any given point. On a $3 million base, that's roughly $72,000 more cash tied up in receivables alone before the growth revenue arrives.

The pattern is predictable. The tightest cash period usually falls five to ten weeks after growth activity accelerates. For most east coast businesses, that lands in mid-August: after July's superannuation payment, after award wage increases in early payrolls, and while June debtors are still collecting.

Knowing the gap is coming — and when — changes what you can do about it. You can approach your bank for facility headroom before the pressure arrives, accelerate collections from your existing debtors, or phase growth to match your cash position.

ProfitPulse works with owner-led SMEs across Queensland, New South Wales, and Victoria to build the forward cash model that makes this timing visible before it becomes a problem.

Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.
